Journal of Vegetation Science 10: 631-640, 1999
© IAVS; Opulus Press Uppsala. Printed in Sweden
                                   - On the identification of optimal plant functional types -                             631


              On the identification of optimal plant functional types

                                                   Pillar, Valério DePatta

     Departamento de Ecologia, Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Sul, Porto Alegre, 91540-000, Brazil;
                                       Fax +55513191568; E-mail vpillar@ecologia.ufrgs.br




Abstract. The study of vegetation response to environmental         some instances so broad and variable that by describing
change on a global scale cannot rely on species because most        communities by species composition we may not per-
plant species have geographically limited distributions. To         ceive relevant patterns occurring below the resolving
allow ecological predictions beyond the scale of the floristic
                                                                    power of species (Díaz et al. 1992). The problem has
region, models have to rely on vegetation descriptions using
plant types other than the species. The crucial problem is how
                                                                    been studied in connection with the IGBP (Steffen et al.
to define the types. Since types are described by traits, the       1992), where these types are designated as ‘plant func-
problem translates into one of optimal trait selection. The best    tional types’ (PFTs).
plant traits are those that when used to define plant types              It is well known that regions of the world with
optimize the perception of association between vegetation and       similar climates tend to support structurally similar veg-
environmental (e.g., climate, disturbance) variation. I con-        etation (e.g. Naveh 1967). This led to the proposition
sider trait selection as a two-step procedure. The first step is    that community evolution is convergent (e.g. Mooney &
the selection of a larger trait set based on past experience and    Dunn 1970; Barbour & Diaz 1973; Orians & Solbrig
known practicality, which is used for community description.
The second step, for which the paper describes new methods,
                                                                    1977a), but suitable analytical techniques were lacking.
is accomplished on the data analytically by suitable computer       The existence of PFTs with global validity is particu-
algorithms that can find the optimal subset among the pre-          larly grounded on this hypothesis.
selected traits. This subset defines optimal plant functional            There are different approaches to analyse vegeta-
types (PFTs). The methods involve a fuzzy set approach and          tion data based on traits or plant types. A prevalent
community description by plant types. The optimization algo-        analytical scheme (e.g. Feoli & Scimone 1984; Díaz et
rithms described are tested with data from plant communities        al. 1992; Díaz & Cabido 1997) is the multiplication of
in South and North America. The utility of the approach in the      the matrix of traits by species by the matrix holding
evaluation of convergence of phylogenetically distant plant
                                                                    presences/absences or performances of the species in
communities is discussed.
                                                                    the sampled sites, yielding a matrix of traits by sites. The
                                                                    latter is then analysed by conventional multivariate meth-
Keywords: Chaco; Climate; Convergence; Fuzzy set; Leaf              ods. A limitation of this approach is that the traits must
functional type; Monte; Optimal PFT set; Sonoran Desert.            be binary or quantitative. A trait which is qualitative
                                                                    multistate may be coded into as many binary traits as the

Introduction                                                        be groups of species defined by cluster analysis of the
                                                                    matrix of species by traits. We may instead define plant
    To describe communities we need a taxonomy to                   types as a combination of trait states, in which case the
dissect the assemblage of organisms into populations.               classification is monothetic. Orlóci (1991) used the term
We take these populations as community components.                  ‘character set type’ for trait combinations. He suggested
Pioneer studies in vegetation recognized plant types by             accordingly an analytical scheme in which the traits are
morphology and function (see Du Rietz 1931 and refer-               considered hierarchically based on a data matrix of
ences therein). Vegetation science has been essentially             character set types by sites (see Orlóci & Orlóci 1985;
based on species, but this view has been criticized (e.g.           Orlóci et al. 1986 and the review by Pillar & Orlóci
Grime 1979; Ghiselin 1987). It is clear why we need                 1993a). The analysis may adopt a fuzzy set approach
vegetation descriptions with plant types defined by traits          (Pillar & Orlóci 1991).
and not only by species. On a large scale, predictions                   The crucial problem, however, is how to select the
based on plant species are geographically bound (Wood-              traits so that the PFTs will likely be ‘functional’. The
ward & Cramer 1996). On a small scale, species are in               traits must be observable expressions of forms or behaviors
632                                                       Pillar, V.D.

defining plant types that are responsive, in terms of              Methods
occurrence or performance, to changes in ecosystem
conditions. A prevalent approach in the search for PFTs            Data acquisition and handling
is the selection of a trait set a priori, which is then used
for description and interpretations thereafter. Box (1981)             Community description is accomplished by record-
for example chooses traits based on perceived form-                ing the presence or more often the quantities of plant
function relationships. Others use selection criteria that         populations. In the present case, the plant populations
are consistent with theoretical principles (Thompson et            are PFTs defined as sets of trait states. A PFT (or any
al. 1996; Grime et al. 1997) or base the selection on              other designation for plant types) is identified as a
known or suspected trends of trait variation in ecologi-           population containing plants that are similar with regard
cal space (e.g. Díaz et al. 1992; Skarpe 1996). Feasibil-          to the pre-selected traits. This ‘population’ does not
ity and cost of observation are also trait selection criteria      necessarily coincide with a species. But, as a conven-
(Skarpe 1996; Díaz & Cabido 1997). Traits may also be              ience for field description, the plants belonging to the
selected because they show correlation with major com-             same species in a community may form a PFT if the
ponents in the ordination space (Thompson et al. 1996;             variability of the traits within that species in that com-
Chapin et al. 1996; Grime et al. 1997), or because they            munity is acceptable. In this case, even if two or more
define plant groups that are stable after different analyti-       species have identical descriptions they may be re-
cal techniques and repeatable in different sites or differ-        corded separately. If the intra-species variability for the
ent times (Gitay & Noble 1997). How functional or                  selected traits is also acceptable across communities,
ecologically relevant are the plant types so derived?              the use of floras or new information on traits (e.g. from
This can be answered by comparing derived ecological               Grime et al. 1997) coupled with community descrip-
trends to empirical evidence (Thompson et al. 1996;                tions from published records and data bases is allowed.
Skarpe 1996; Chapin et al. 1996; Díaz & Cabido 1997;                   The description of the PFTs, accordingly, yields a
Gitay et al. 1999; Kleyer 1999). But, in the most recent           matrix B of PFTs by traits. The description of the
literature on PFTs, checking these trends is a posteriori          communities yields a matrix W of PFTs by sites
and not part of the analytical search for PFTs per se.             holding species presences/absence or quantitative val-
Furthermore, it is clear at the outset that we can hardly          ues. The traits may be binary, qualitative multistate, and
expect to find a set of traits or PFTs that would be
optimal for all purposes. The selected traits may, for
instance, define PFTs that are good indicators for macro-
climatic change but may not be so good for changes in
grazing regime (Skarpe 1996).
    In this paper, I offer an analytical approach for
trait selection, which is complementary to the a priori
approach. By definition, community descriptions
based on PFTs should produce data in which the
compositional variation is highly congruent with en-
vironmental variation. Consider that we have a data
matrix describing plant types by traits that were pre-
selected. Consider also that we have two other matrices,
one describing the communities by the composition of
the plant types and the other the sites by environmental
variables. With these three data matrices we can evalu-
ate ecological relevance of the traits and accordingly we
can rank the traits. Based on this we can choose an
optimal subset of the initial trait set. I discuss in the
sequel a general approach for the analysis, describe in
some detail algorithms for finding optimal PFTs, and
describe examples using data from the Chaco and Monte
formations of Argentina and the Sonoran Desert of the              Fig. 1. A general algorithm for the selection of an optimal
United States. The specific methodology has evolved                subset of traits to maximize congruence ρ(D; ∆) of vegetation
from earlier methods described in Pillar & Orlóci (1991,           and environmental variation. Matrix B of PFTs by traits, W of
1993a, b).                                                         PFTs by sites, and E of environmental variables by sites result
                                                                   from description. See the main text for explanation.
                                 - On the identification of optimal plant functional types -                                   633

Optimization of the trait set                                     an initial k = 16, there will be 65 535 different trait subsets

    The optimization algorithm is based on the magni-             algorithm.
tude of the congruence between vegetational variation
and environmental variation. To measure this I use a              Fuzzy PFTs
matrix correlation ρ (D; ∆), similarly as a standardized
Mantel (1967) statistic. D is a dissimilarity matrix of sites          If the notion of identity is absolute, no matter how
computed from matrix X of PFTs by sites (explained                similar two PFTs might be, they are not to be pooled
below). ∆ is a dissimilarity matrix of the same sites based       unless their records are exactly identical regarding the
on environmental data (matrix E). Any dissimilarity func-         selected traits. This may aggravate the problem of ana-
tions are applicable for D and ∆. I define ρ as the               lytical indeterminacy caused by an excessive number of
Pearson's product moment correlation involving n (n – 1)          absences (zeros) in community comparisons (Pillar &
/2 off-diagonal pairs of corresponding dissimilarities in         Orlóci 1993a: 80). An alternative is offered by the fuzzy
D and ∆. The larger ρ (D; ∆), the more likely that the            approach (Pillar & Orlóci 1991, 1993a). The theory of
PFTs are in fact ‘functional’ for the environmental               fuzzy sets was proposed by Zadeh (1965) and has been
factors taken into account.                                       applied in ecology (e.g. Roberts 1986, 1989; Feoli &
    Consider that a description of communities used k             Zuccarello 1988; Marsili-Libelli 1989; Banyikwa et al.

subset of the pre-selected trait set may suffice to define        by a degree of membership. Only if the object belongs
optimal PFTs. This is the foundation of the optimization          entirely to a set (degree = 1) or not at all (degree = 0), the set
algorithm (Fig. 1). A short numerical example is pre-             is called an ordinary (crisp) set. In fuzzy sets the degree of
sented in App. 1. Accordingly, matrix C, initially with s         membership is between 0 and 1.
PFTs by m traits, is extracted from matrix B. Similarly,               The fuzzy approach will take the partial similarity of
matrix X with s PFTs by n sites is extracted from matrix          PFTs into account (Fig. 2). From matrix Ctxm a symmetric
W. Identical PFTs may appear since m ≤ k. Pooling of              matrix Utxt is computed, which contains similarities on
identical PFTs will leave t distinct PFTs in matrix C, with       the 0 to 1 scale for all pairs of the t distinct PFTs based on
corresponding quantities in matrix X. With these elements         the m traits. Any similarity measure is acceptable. But,
and matrix ∆ the congruence ρ (D; ∆) is evaluated.                when the traits are of mixed types (e.g. qualitative and
634                                                      Pillar, V.D.

quantitative traits in the same set), indexes proposed by
Goodall (1966) and Gower (1971) may be convenient
choices. The rows in matrix U define fuzzy sets. That is,
row d holds the grades of membership ude of each PFT
e in the fuzzy set formed by PFTs similar to PFT d. All
PFTs belong to this fuzzy set defined by the properties
(trait states) of PFT d, with a certain grade of member-
ship varying from 0 to 1. Only PFT d though has udd =
1 to this fuzzy set since only distinct PFTs originate
matrix U. The elements in U may be adjusted by u ζde ,
                                                         –1


where 0 > ξ ≤ 1 expresses a degree of fuzziness chosen
by the researcher. If ξ is one, there will be no change in
the fuzzy set. If ξ approaches zero, ude will be zero if the
similarity ude < 1 and one if ude = 1, that is, the set will
become not-fuzzy (crisp) and U will be an identity                Fig. 2. A fuzzy set approach for the analysis of community
matrix.                                                           data described by plant types (Pillar & Orlóci 1991, 1993a).

or leaf thickness to increase on gradients of decreasing           floras. That is, first, optimal trait subsets for precipita-

Solbrig 1977b). Compound leaves favor deciduousness                mal trait subset for each one data set was validated using
and show increased frequency with aridity. Modified                the other data set in isolation or jointly in terms of
leaf surfaces (glaucous, trichomous) increase leaf re-             congruence ρ(D; ∆) under the same fuzziness level. The
flectance and are more common in sunnier or arid sites.            analyses were performed with the software SYNCSA

pathway. Thorniness appears to be related to herbivore             adjustments, resemblance measures, ranking of traits
avoidance. Biological type encompasses a suit of other             and ordination. An early version of this software is
traits, such as growth form, leaf type, photosynthetic             described by Pillar & Orlóci (1993a).
pathway. Plant height reflects carbon immobilization in
636                                                            Pillar, V.D.

Results

    The analysis of the Chaco/Monte data (Fig. 3) found
optimal subsets of traits for response to the rainfall gradi-
ent. The optimal subset with crisp PFTs, in both stepwise
and full algorithms, was formed by ‘leaf size’ only: ρ (D;
∆) = 0.806). This allowed the definition of five PFTs. It is
important to note that PFTs defined using the complete
set of 14 traits revealed a much lower environmental
congruence value: ρ (D; ∆) = 0.545, (Table 2). For valida-
tion, this optimal trait subset was used to define the PFTs
in the analysis of the Arizona stands, which resulted in a
close congruence with precipitation variation: ρ (D; ∆) =
0.788. A joint analysis of the stands from Argentina and
Arizona, described by similarly defined PFTs, also re-
vealed a close congruence with precipitation variation: ρ
(D; ∆) = 0.796. Furthermore, the stands analysed jointly
showed a clear, though non-linear vegetation pattern in
ordination space (Fig. 4a). Also, the PFTs on the same
ordination space are separated between smaller and larger
leaf-size classes. An ordination with fuzzy PFTs portrays
a more linear trend, from drier sites, characterized by
small leaf sizes, to moister sites characterized by larger
leaf sizes (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c depicts the joint ordination of
sites from both areas described by crisp PFTs defined by                  Fig. 3. Profile of maximum congruence ρ(D; ∆) at different
the suboptimal, complete set of pre-selected traits. The                  trait subset sizes, with crisp (a) and fuzzy (b) PFTs. The data
optimal subset of traits for fuzzy PFTs (Fig. 3b) contains                include 19 sites from the Chaco and Monte. ∆ is based on
‘leaf size’, ‘leaf dorsal hairiness’, ‘leaf texture’ and ‘leaf            precipitation data. Maximum congruence found by the full
succulence’. The analysis revealed a slightly lower congru-               algorithm is at the solid line. The dotted line indicates maxi-
                                                                          mum congruence found by the stepwise algorithm. Vegetation
ence in validation than the one for crisp PFTs (Table 2).
                                                                          composition is defined by plant types based on traits, which
    The sequence of optimization was then reversed, by                    for the stepwise algorithm are shown on the horizontal axis
finding an optimal trait subset for the Arizona data, which               cumulatively from right to left (see labels in Table 1). Note
was in turn used in similar analyses as above involving                   that when subset size = 14, the complete trait set is used. When
the Chaco/Monte sites. See Table 2. Although the                          the lines meet the trait subset is identical in both algorithms.
congruence in the Arizona data set was very high, it                      Data from Pillar (1992).




Fig. 4. Principal coordinates analysis based on chord distances for 29 sites from Chaco (c), Monte (m) and Arizona (u). Numbers
identify the average annual precipitation at the sites (scale 1 - 9). a, b. sites described by five PFTs based on ‘leaf size’. PFTs t1-t5, in
italics, from smaller to larger leaf sizes, plotted by their correlation (rescaled) with the axes. The PFTs in a. are crisp, in b. fuzzy. c.
sites described by 223 crisp PFTs defined by all 14 traits. Data from Pillar (1992) and L. & M. Orlóci (unpubl.).
                                      - On the identification of optimal plant functional types -                                  637

algorithm, with different data sets and degrees of fuzziness,            with Arizona and Chaco/Monte data sets. Other definitions are
DF, (crisp, C: ξ approaching 0, fuzzy, F: ξ = 1). ρ(D; ∆) is the         in the caption of Table 2.
congruence of vegetation and precipitation variation consider-           Trait              Congruence ρ (D;∆)
ing all pre-selected traits or only the optimal trait subset. The                Arizona Chaco/Monte             Arizona   Chaco/Monte
‘optimal trait subset’ was found by the optimization algorithm
                                                                         bt       0.856    – 0.101        st      0.722      – 0.104
aiming at maximal congruence ρ(D; ∆). The elements in D are
                                                                         fu       0.904      0.216        es      0.727        0.443
chord distances of sites and in ∆ the absolute differences of            ls       0.852      0.242        su      0.259      – 0.077
annual precipitation between the sites. ‘ρ(D; ∆) in validation’          tx       0.838      0.538        sh      0.751        0.351
is the congruence, based on PFTs defined by the optimal trait            ar       0.724      0.313        ed      0.793        0.672
subset, in the analysis (a) with the other data set (Arizona or          ev       0.844      0.664        si      0.788        0.806
                                                                         th       0.855      0.393        he      0.955        0.281
Chaco/Monte) or (b) with Chaco/Monte/Arizona jointly, to
which ‘Number of PFTs’ refers. Results of the full algorithm
were identical to the ones presented, except in the cases in             of fuzziness into the analysis is a decision analogous to
which they are indicated in parentheses.

             traits                 traits     a         b    a      b   correlation between community and environmental

                                                                         stand is absolute, while in the fuzzy approach absence is
Arizona C      0.933   he ed st       0.980   0.470   0.601   35   46
                       (he ed at sh) 0.980    0.502   0.573   44   87
                                                                         a matter of degree and zeros may be nearly eliminated
Arizona F      0.845   he ls tx su    0.945   0.422   0.539   40   76    from the data table (Pillar & Orlóci 1991). As a high
Joint sets C   0.498   si sh ar su th 0.873       -       -    -   31    degree of analytical indeterminacy is linked to non-lin-
           F   0.637   si he          0.860       -       -    -   33    earity, the fuzzy approach may reduce non-linearity in the
                                                                         data. However, an analysis with fuzzy PFTs conveys to
                                                                         the community data (by matrix multiplication) the simi-
dropped to much lower values when validated with the                     larities of the PFTs based on the traits. Consequently,

mal (Fig. 4c), but greater with fuzzy than with crisp PFTs.              optimal trait subset with fuzzy PFTs for the Chaco/

Discussion                                                               when the traits ‘leaf dorsal hairiness’, ‘leaf texture’ and
                                                                         ‘leaf succulence’ are added to the subset containing only
    The results suggest that the technique works. There                  ‘leaf size’ (Fig. 3b). Therefore, in this case there is little
seems to be no advantage in using the more computa-                      advantage in defining PFTs by the three traits in addi-
tionally demanding full optimization algorithm instead of                tion to ‘leaf size’. The optimal trait subsets revealed by
the stepwise algorithm. Both found nearly identical opti-                the Arizona analysis also contain redundancies. In this
mal PFTs. When solutions diverged it was at suboptimal                   case, there was, e.g. a tiny increase (from 0.976 to
subsets or when the alternative subsets compared at a                    0.980) in the congruence when the trait ‘stem tissue
given subset size had very close congruence values.                      type’ was included in the subset with ‘plant height’ and
    The results of optimization with crisp and fuzzy                     ‘leaf dorsal hairiness’. In fact, due to redundancy among
plant types differed. Whether to build or not the notion                 traits in this data set, different subsets of traits were very

described by Pillar & Orlóci (1993a).                                  Thompson et al. 1996; Chapin et al. 1996; Grime et al.

dated with the communities in Arizona, but the reverse                 environmental data were not explicitly considered in the
was not so. All traits were nearly equally relevant defin-             analysis seeking PFTs, as in the present paper. Gitay &

tion for the Arizona data set, but only some of the traits             stable after different analytical techniques, with differ-
were good for the Chaco/Monte data set. Especially the                 ent traits, and repeatable at different sites or different
congruence for PFTs defined by ‘plant height’ was                      times. However, ecologically relevant PFTs may not
much greater for the Arizona data set. It is not clear                 necessarily comply with these conditions. PFTs may be
whether the fact that the gradient in Arizona encom-                   fuzzy in terms of the defining traits but show consistent
passes much greater species richness would explain                     responses to changes in ecosystem conditions in differ-
these results, for most PFTs of one region in general                  ent floristic regions. Furthermore, the fact that the groups
tended to have counterparts in PFTs of the other region.               are repeatable at different sites is not sufficient to con-
This may explain why ‘biological type’, which is a                     clude that they form similar community patterns at the
complex of other traits, was not relevant for the Chaco/               different sites. Repeatability is better defined in the
Monte data set. It is clear, however, that the approach                same way as I defined community convergence, that is,
involving optimization followed by cross validation is                 the consistency of community trends or responses, in
capable of seeking traits that are optimal in more than                terms of PFTs, to similar gradients in different sites.
one floristic region. This is the case of ‘leaf size’, which
determined high congruence with both data sets. Inci-                  Acknowledgements. Grants and fellowships from CNPQ,
dentally, the relation between ‘leaf size’ and rainfall                CAPES and FAPERGS (Brazil) supported the research. I
gradients is well known (e.g. Givnish 1987).                           recognize the important contribution of L. Orlóci in the devel-
    The results highlight the potential for analytical op-             opment of the ideas presented here, and thank him and M.
timization in PFT-based vegetation analysis. The results               Orlóci for permission to use their data. I am very grateful to
clearly show that a selection of traits from an initial set,           J.P. Lewis for invaluable cooperation in data collection in
may improve the ecological relevance of the PFTs.                      Argentina. S. Lavorel, S. Hartz, S. Díaz and an anonymous
                                                                       reviewer gave many helpful suggestions to improve the paper.
However, a priori selection of traits is critical, because
the optimization will be useless if the traits are irrelevant
at the scale of the study and intended objectives. In this             References
regard, analytical optimization is an important comple-
mentary tool. Whether PFTs are really functional should                Banyikwa, F.F., Feoli, E. & Zuccarello, V. 1990. Fuzzy set
not be a question of definition, but something that can be                ordination and classification of Serengeti short grasslands,
decided by measurement in a given data set.                               Tanzania. J. Veg. Sci. 1: 97-104.
    Due to phylogenetic constraints (Givnish 1987), or-                Barbour, M.G. & Diaz, D.V. 1973. Larrea plant communities
ganisms tend to evolve towards possessing common                          on bajada and moisture gradients in the United States and
                                                                          Argentina. Vegetatio 28: 335-352.
sets of attributes. It is not difficult to find plant traits that
                                                                       Box, E.O. 1981. Macroclimate and plant forms. Junk, The Hague.

App. 1. Numerical example illustrating steps of the PFT-optimization algorithm. Data set with three sites using PFTs defined by pre-
selected traits: leaf size, si; leaf life span, ls; plant height, he (Table 1) and precipitation as environmental variable. See main text.

Precipitation in mm: R1 = 168; R2 = 360; R3 = 700.                                           Matrix D (squared chord distances) based on fuzzy (or crisp) PFTs:
Environmental distance matrix ∆ (squared Euclidean distances):                               0           1.63148 0.686936
0    192       532                                                                                       0         0.772119
     0         340                                                                                                 0
               0                                                                             Congruence with PFTs defined by ‘ls’: Fuzzy: – 0.86952; Crisp: – 0.86952.
Vegetation data with 5 PFTs defined by the pre-selected traits (matrix B), with
corresponding quantities in the sites (matrix W):                                            3. Trait subset is formed by plant height (he):
PFT si ls he           R1 R2 R3                                                                   Similar steps as in (1), matrix C and B extracted and congruence computed
                                                                                             accordingly.
1      1 5 3              2 4 0                                                              Congruence with PFTs defined by ‘he’: Fuzzy: 0.930836; Crisp: 0.359799.
2      2 2 6              0 5 3                                                              The trait that, when used alone, is maximizing congruence is plant height, if using
3      4 4 6              0 0 3                                                              fuzzy PFTs, and leaf size, if using crisp PFTs.
4      4 2 5              0 2 0
5      3 4 4              5 0 0                                                              The next steps evaluate subsets of two traits. The full algorithm evaluates all
Then, different trait subsets extracted from the data above are used to define PFTs          combinations of two traits, the stepwise evaluates some of the subsets
and examined for congruence ρ (D; ∆). It starts with one-trait subsets:
                                                                                             41. Trait subset is formed by leaf size (si) and leaf life span (ls):
1 Trait subset is formed by leaf size (si):                                                        Vegetation data (matrices C and X) extracted from matrices B and W.
      Vegetation data (matrices C and X) extracted from matrices B and W, with               PFT       si ls        R1 R2 R3
PFTs defined by leaf size. Note that PFTs 3 and 4 were pooled since they are                 1         1 5          2 4 0
identical for leaf size.                                                                     2         2 2          0 5 3
                                                                                             3         4 4          0 0 3
PFT si                    R1 R2 R3                                                           4         4 2          0 2 0
1        1                2 4 0                                                              5         3 4          5 0 0
2        2                0 5 3
3        4                0 2 3                                                              PFT-similarity matrix U based on leaf size and leaf life span. The similarity of, e.g.
4        3                5 0 0                                                              PFTs 2 and 3 is {[1 – |2 – 4| ÷ (4-1)] + 0 }÷ 3 = (0.333333 + 0) ÷ 2 = 0.166667, where
                                                                                             0.333333 is the similarity for leaf size and zero is the similarity for leaf life span (the
PFT similarity matrix U (Gower index) based on leaf size; a quantitative trait, hence
                                                                                             PFTs do not coincide for leaf life span). The Gower similarity is the average of the
similarity of, e.g. PFTs 2 and 3 is [1 – |2 – 4| ÷ (4 – 1)] = 0.333333, where (4 – 1) =
                                                                                             partial indexes.
leaf size range.
                                                                                             1            0.333333 0                    0            0.166667
1           0.666667 0                0.333333
                                                                                             0.333333 1                  0.166667 0.666667 0.333333
0.666667 1               0.333333 0.666667
                                                                                             0            0.166667 1                    0.5          0.833333
0           0.333333 1                0.666667
                                                                                             0            0.666667 0.5                  1            0.333333
0.333333 0.666667 0.666667 1
                                                                                             0.166667 0.333333 0.833333 0.333333 1
Matrix product Y = UX. In this, e.g. quantity of PFT 2 in R1 = (0.666667 × 2 + 1
× 0 + 0.333333 × 0 + 0.666667 × 5) = 4.666669.                                               Fuzzy PFT-vegetation matrix V after computation of matrix Y = UX, adjustment by site

                                                                                             obviously identical with matrices B and W.

The PFT similarity matrix U based on leaf life span. The only trait is qualitative, thus
                                                                                             In conclusion, for this data set, congruence ρ (D; ∆) is maximized by using PFTs
there are no identical PFTs and all pair-wise similarities are zero.
                                                                                             defined by leaf size and plant height, in both the fuzzy and crisp approaches, and
1            0           0                                                                   with full or stepwise algorithm.
0            1           0
0            0           1                                                                   1crisp PFTs; 2fuzzy PFTs.

Since U is an identity matrix, matrices Y and V are identical with matrix X.
